title: Catalytic advantage in asymptotic entanglement manipulation
abstract: Entanglement is a key quantum resource in various quantum protocols, with a rich set of laws governing its manipulation. In this context, catalysis refers to the possibility of an auxiliary state that enables a previously forbidden manipulation, while being completely returned at the end. While the catalytic setting has been thoroughly examined in the single-copy regime, much less is known in the asymptotically many copy regime. In this work, we focus on the entanglement cost of preparing asymptotically many copies of a given state exactly. We show that catalysis can significantly lower the exact entanglement cost by constructing an explicit catalytic protocol. Additionally, these findings generalize readily to other resource theories, showing a general catalytic advantage in the resource dilution task.
summary: This paper demonstrates that using a catalyst can significantly reduce the amount of entanglement needed to prepare many copies of a mixed state exactly. It establishes that this catalytic advantage in the asymptotic regime is fundamentally linked to the non-convexity of the exact entanglement cost. The findings are shown to be generalizable to other resource theories, such as the thermodynamic cost of work.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether catalysis (using an auxiliary state that is returned unchanged) can provide a significant advantage in reducing the exact entanglement cost in the asymptotic (many-copy) regime.
main_result: The author proves that catalysis can strictly lower the exact entanglement cost for mixed states in both PPT and LOCC frameworks, establishing that this advantage is a direct consequence of the non-convexity of the exact cost.
method: The study uses the PPT entanglement theory as a proxy for LOCC, develops an explicit catalytic protocol using a SWAP operation, and employs mathematical tools like Jensen's Theorem and the properties of max-relative entropy.
model_or_system: The work focuses on entanglement manipulation of mixed states, specifically Werner states and states with positive binegativity, using Bell pairs as the standard resource unit.
key_observables: Exact entanglement cost (E0c), catalytic exact entanglement cost (E0c,c), logarithmic negativity (LN), and exact work cost (W0c).
important_parameters: Asymptotic (many-copy) regime, single-copy regime, strict vs. correlated catalysis, and the distinction between exact and approximate cost regimes.
main_assumptions: The advantage is analyzed in the 'exact' regime; the paper notes that the advantage may disappear in the 'approximate' regime where the cost function becomes convex.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates a catalytic protocol involving a sequence where a system state is transformed via a map followed by a SWAP operation with a catalyst.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of asymptotic catalysis, develops a mathematical framework using PPT and LOCC, provides an explicit protocol construction, proves the link between non-convexity and catalytic advantage, and generalizes the findings to other resource theories like thermodynamics.
why_interesting: It provides fundamental insights into the irreversibility and resource manipulation limits in quantum information, specifically showing how auxiliary systems can fundamentally change the scaling of resource costs in the many-copy limit.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
